summary: During dental trauma involving tipped teeth, not all periodontal ligament (PDL) fibers are equally affected; instead, they exhibit both compression and tension based on their attachment sites relative to the applied force. Surgical interventions further support this variability as different parts of the PDL respond uniquely under such conditions. To summarize concisely for your query regarding what happens specifically when a tooth tips due to external pressure, it is accurate to say these movements cause certain areas within the PDL to become either more tightly packed together ("compressed") whereas other sections may elongate slightly ("stretched"). Key takeaway here being non-uniform response among various fibrous components constituting overall structure supporting our selected answer choice reflecting reality observed clinically post-trauma
